---
title: "ACCEPT Lab"
body-classes: home
---

::: {.hero}
<h1 class="hero-title">ACCEPT Lab</h1>
<p class="hero-subtitle">Causal research for social questions that matter</p>
:::

ACCEPT Lab is a research group within the [School of Psychological Sciences](https://www.wgtn.ac.nz/psyc) at Te Herenga Waka - Victoria University of Wellington, and is affiliated with the [Centre for Applied Cross-Cultural Research (CACR)](https://www.wgtn.ac.nz/cacr).

## Why Causality?

Does participation in an after-school tutoring programme *cause* better marks? Simple observation might show participants have higher scores, but those students might also be more motivated or have more supportive families. Distinguishing intervention effects from background differences is the central challenge of evaluation. At ACCEPT Lab, we use causal inference and machine learning to estimate whether interventions work, for whom they work, and under which conditions. This work supports clearer decisions in psychological science and public policy.

## Why Culture?

Policy effects vary across cultural settings. Religious identity, belief, practice, and community structure can change both exposure to interventions and responses to them. Studying variation in religion is therefore essential for external validity and responsible inference. ACCEPT Lab combines causal methods with cross-cultural evidence to explain when findings travel across groups and when they do not.

## About the Lab

ACCEPT Lab studies causal questions in psychology and the social sciences, with a particular interest in culture, religion, and social life.

We use observational and experimental data to examine how interventions, institutions, and social environments shape behaviour and wellbeing. Our work also includes teaching and supervision in causal inference.

## What We Study

### Processes and Mechanisms in Social Life

We study the social and psychological processes that shape collective life: the formation and erosion of trust, including institutional trust; the conditions under which people volunteer; the ways personality, institutions, and social inequality shape wellbeing and behaviour; and the role of meaning and religion in organising behaviour across communities and over time.

When those problems require causal explanation, we use observational and longitudinal causal inference to ask what changes what, for whom, and under what conditions. ACCEPT Lab has been part of an early generation of laboratories bringing these tools into psychological and social science, using approaches such as doubly robust estimation, heterogeneous treatment effect analysis, and longitudinal intervention models where they materially improve inference.

### Places Map Infrastructure

We maintain [Places Map](https://www.placesmap.org/), an open research infrastructure for studying places of worship and cultural context at scale. The project supports reproducible spatial analysis, new collaborations, and public scholarship. Code and data workflows are maintained in the [places-of-worship repository](https://github.com/go-bayes/places-of-worship).

### Application Domains

Our current work includes social inequality, volunteering and charity, institutional trust, political psychology, personality, health and wellbeing, meaning and purpose, spirituality, and religion.

## Recent Work

Visit our [Presentations](presentations.qmd) section to explore recent research talks and workshops.

## Explore the Lab

Visit our [about](about.qmd) page for methods, current projects, and contact details.

## Contact

If you would like to ask about the lab's work, please email.

[Email: joseph.bulbulia@vuw.ac.nz](mailto:joseph.bulbulia@vuw.ac.nz){.btn .btn-primary}

---

::: {.callout-note}
## Updates
Stay updated with our latest research, publications, and events in the [news](news.qmd) section.
:::
